item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image originates from here. It's not compliant with CC 3.0 and as such has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, the title is supposed to be catchy by definition, and generally in an entry like this it's normal that if the title isn't immediatly clear right at the start (which, arguably, this is. Hell, they hunt children and turn 'em into something like themselves. They are the "Adults" as opposed to the "children" even without considering normal humans vs abnormal ones) it will be at the end.

And if you think about it as a member of the Foundation you don't get a title, it's just "1788".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe this has been mentioned up thread and I overlooked it; but this immediately makes me think of Larry Niven's Ringworld stories.
Aside from the specific anatomy1, this could be a direct port of his Protectors. The unknown process inflicted on children could easily be force feeding them tree-of-life.

Bonus: in googling just now to remind myself what Niven's critters were called, I learned that he first published the concept in a short story titled "The Adults".

No accusation of plagiarism, I think it's just a strong example of converging development. And a unique enough spin on the concept to hold up on its own even if it was a deliberate skip-ification.

Footnotes
1. which is established in Niven-verse to be highly sub-species variable anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Immediately thought of w40k genestealers, especially when the bonus hidden arms came in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this. One of the things I particularly like is how it evokes the fear of preying upon innocence while simultaneously sidestepping (via lampshade) the whole gross 'the monster is a pedophile!' angle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If there's one thing that kills my enjoyment of a skip, it's reading a lengthy "credentials required" section only to find out the big twist at the end was literally the title, I.E. the thing I read before I even clicked on it. It's like solving a Chinese puzzle box and the only thing inside it is a slip of paper that says "Made in China."

I want my curiosity back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I stumbled across this today and initially though it was pretty intriguing. For some reason, what really snagged me was the final line of the interview "Grow up. Or die."

I also just finished watching the movie Religulous and the final spoken line by Bill Maher is "Grow up or die."

I can't see any sensible link between the two and I'm chalking it up to coincidence, but it at least made me come back to read it again for a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here, i think the interview serves to reinforce the article. I'd rather it stay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This spooked me pretty badly.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really? I just read through and liked it. Reinforces the psychopathic nature of the creatures.

Good dialogue is hard to write, but I thought this was fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last interview makes it go from "omg!" to "omg! what was he thinking?!". You take a wonderful, well thought and executed scip and add it a totally unnecesary cackling maniac like interview that ruins the mood and the mistery. Please, please just take it out. You don't even need to replace it, just delete it.

Downvoted

item.title: Re: Creepy and unsettling...
item.content:encoded: No he probably meant SCP-542 aka Herr Chirurg

item.title: Re: Creepy and unsettling...
item.content:encoded: By "chirurg", he meant "surgeon".

item.title: Creepy and unsettling...
item.content:encoded: …and I don't suppose there's any way to let Chirurg take a peek inside one of them, is there? He might have insights to offer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminds me of "Blindsight" vampires.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Super-necro-posting fifth? To be fair, it is at +100, but for a skip this old and this good, it deserves higher rating.
Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's exactly what I thought. I like this in general, but it would work much better without the extra arms. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The true reason for TSA's security changes.

item.title: I really like this SCP, but....
item.content:encoded: There are some issues I noticed. It says they're radically different from "normal"adults, but having four arms kind of detracts from that sentiment, especially if the extra arms are larger or the same size as the "primary" pair. That's just something I saw that was kind of confusing, cause I'd imagine they'd move around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"hard to distinguish upon close inspection"
yet has a second fucking pair of arms?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fourthed. I feel this concept would be hard to effectively pull off, and this skip is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thirded. It's a unique idea based around something that could actually happen, and not OMG MAJICK.
I also loved the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This deserves to be much, much higher rated than it actually is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna be honest, I really don't like this idea. It seems like something lifted straight from Codename: Kids Next Door. And for that reason, a hesitant -1. The writing is decent, however, and this interested me enough the read through, but I just dislike this concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I pretty much liked this when I saw "Adults", "Keter", and the weird face. Another human evolution. Can't say I disliked the original O5 note. Nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one, some tone issues aside. It's creepy, and inspires thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has evidently been fixed extensively, and as such I am upvoting; I recommend that all those who haven't been back to see the difference do the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or clowns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Naked photos of children were relatively common in that era, I think.

And Dodgson's heirs purged his diaries of everything that they believed could cause a scandal; it's generally accepted that they removed everything that indicated interest in adult women (because he was unmarried, you see). Classic backfire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Louis Carroll took nude photographs. But with the permission of the children and the children's parents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is much improved by the change from note to interview. I still think the physical changes are slightly bigger than I'd like for the concept, but overall I like it now.

Even though I still don't get the feeling that he actually is an adult version, I like that he believes that. And I get the feeling that believing that, and accepting it, may be part of the transformation. Or even part of what causes it.

upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another delightfully spooky creature contained by the Foundation.

I wonder how insulted they'd be if I called them savage animals…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't seen the original post and I like this. Those rewrites have worked. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I'm surprised I'm doing this, but the total replacement of the O5/Site Director note with the interview changes my view on this article enough that I'm cancelling my downvote. I think you did a good job with it, and it's definitely the right choice for the article.

Also, I like that it leaves room for ambiguity. The article doesn't sell me that these things are … what they say they are, but it certainly tells me that they believe it.

And they have the ability to act on their beliefs in a tangible way either way, and if they get enough resources they could do some real serious shit (another SCP-1000 connection! ;)). All that is seriously disturbing.

I'm still not sure on your execution of the humanoids themselves. They're still just evil four-armed fat people. Hm.

I think I'd prefer it if you tried a different take on the four-armed fat people. I'd like to be able to suspend my disbelief enough to wonder if these fuckers are right. But the way they are now, I can't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview changed my neutral vote into a +1. Good work there. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview is better than the researcher note, but I still heavily dislike the radical anatomy changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better. I'd previously downvoted due to kingreapers posts pointing out properly what was wrong with the article that I'd sorta noticed, but this makes it decent enough to remove the downvote. I'm still confused about the reaction to the accusation of pedophilia, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, I thought I'd caught all those. There's only supposed to be SCP-1788 (the process) and 1788-1 (the people). The -2s are left over from the last version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Current designations are a mess. You call the same thing SCP-1788, SCP-1788-1 and SCP-1788-2 in different parts of the description. If I am understanding correctly, SCP-1788 is a process that transforms children into SCP-1788-1, and SCP-1788-1 are four-armed fat people. I have no idea what SCP-1788-2 are.

I like it, now that all the bad stuff have been removed. Fix the designation thing, and I'll upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's no way I can swallow this concept as a main-list SCP. Tweaked the right way I can maybe see a 001 here, but in it's present form the way the Foundation is handling it doesn't make any sense to me. The revelation that these things are our "natural" state is significant enough that I would expect consideraply more action than jurt main-list classification and a coverup. Moreover, although I won't dispute the fact that the O5 would want to keep the twist secret, particularly if they DID decide just to sit on the problem for whatever reason, but the deception here is excessively elaborate and too easy to disprove (eg, there ARE no implants).

I can't say I'm overly fond of the execution either, but ultimately I can't see any way I could swallow this concept. Humanity-as-SCP has too many in-universe repercussions to work, especially if those repercussions are not addressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Figured out a way to fix this bad boy, but it's going to take me a few hours. Come back later—hopefully you'll like the new version better?

Fixed! Or changed, at least. Please reread and tell me if you think it's an improvement. I certainly think it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if there really are augments being made to the -1 subjects by an unknown instigator, or if the -1 subjects never really were human in the first place, merely human-like beings whose larval forms resemble normal humans?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 'hidden truth' is enough to prevent me from downvoting, but the flaws in that hidden truth are enough to keep me from upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's good, but I think it's a bit too long. The concept (while fairly unoriginal but still unexpected and enjoyable) is sound, but I reckon the addendum at the bottom doesn't work too well. If I were you, I'd work on a way to get it to work in the description rather than relying on extra information.

Hmm, also, how do you know they're our "fully evolved" forms? How do you know they're not separate entities trying to upgrade us, Cyberman style? Also, if there's no downside to being an enhanced human… why bother stopping them?

Congratulations on making me faintly scared of fat people, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My advice would be to replace the site director going "they say they're us when we grow up, so we believe them" with an interview transcript.

I can push my SoD to the point where I'll go "Maybe they are an adult state?" but not quite far enough to go "And of course a site director would believe them".

Personally that would change my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What a tweeest!
The jell-o scientific side of the whole deal kinda puts me off. I know little of biology but I still went it doesn't work liek that a few times throughout.

I kinda dig the concept. Unoriginal but apparently the author didnt know so no loss of virtue there on his part. It does deter on 1788 itself though.

Neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is generally well written (ignoring some improbable bits and kitschiness), but I hate cover-story-twist SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a kernel of a good idea here but the execution is way off. In particular, any SCP which has to use a note from an O5 or researcher to explain the concept had better be phenomenal, otherwise it just looks like you couldn't figure out how to get the idea across in the regular SCP format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… 05 went to Wayland's School of Biology? This whole article reads like everything that was wrong with Prometheus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, thanks to all of you for the suggestions. I really wish I'd known about Niven's work before writing this, because it sounds like more than a few people are pissed that my version is both similar to and less scientific than his, which … I dunno, guys. I'm an English major, this is all the science you're gettin'.

I changed the containment procedures along VAElynx's suggestion, because it's definitely better than what I had (although I did like tying into the States' recent anti-obesity measures). Thanks, VAElynx!

I also added several notes in the note to explain that most of what the Foundation knows comes from these people—which is the reason for the ear protection in the containment measures and the lie about hypnotic voices, as well. I tried to hint at that, but I didn't do a very good job of it.

Lastly, I took thedeadlymoose's (and others') suggestion and changed the writer of the note from an O5 to the Site Director. That's more what I was aiming for, originally, but I wanted something that carried enough weight, and I guess the idea of the Site Director never occurred to me. Of course, now I'm going to be compared even more to SCP-1000, but I still feel like it's a change for the better, at least.

Man, and I was so sure this one was going to take off. At least it's better than Jester, amirite?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh. It'd be interesting if the O5 got it wrong and this was someone's attempt to regulate human population by trimming out what he considers refuse - hence apex predator derived from humans that can camouflage as the sort of person you would expect to see in the surroundings of its prey.

I would totally buy this, if any indications were in the article.

But even if they were, it would make the O5 come off even stupider than he/she already comes off, and it doesn't live up to the original twist… so I dunno.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. It'd be interesting if the O5 got it wrong and this was someone's attempt to regulate human population by trimming out what he considers refuse - hence apex predator derived from humans that can camouflage as the sort of person you would expect to see in the surroundings of its prey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is amazing. All I can think is, "Michelle Obama is an entrenched Foundation agent!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This format is pretty identical to what I did with SCP-1000 (right down to the explanatory note in dramatic fashion revealing that the above stuff is a lie) except with the Larry Niven twist (right down to a missing mechanism to transform to the final form).

I do really like the twist. But I don't like what you did with it.

First, some nitpicks I'm uncertain about.

The execution of the article feels forced to me. Trouble is, I'm not sure if I feel it's forced because it's so similar to what I did except more over the top (and I am astonished that you managed to get it more over the top, and will address why I think this is the case momentarily) or whether it actually is forced. For example, why make it an O5 note? While we're comparing it to 1000, there's a reason I didn't choose an O5 to sign off on it. It felt a little wanky to me, a way to further puff up an already puffed up article. Again, this is another place where my perspective might be a little skewed because I went through a similar thought process to you and came to a different decision.

There's also the issue of the evolutionary record. This is, again, something I put a lot of effort into handwaving for SCP-1000, but which you totally ignore here. If you're gonna say 'we are teh demons' you need to explain why no one has noticed we were teh demons before. Because from an evolutionary perspective, this is total nonsense. Niven bullshits his version pretty good, but you don't. Niven based his Pak's final form on elements of actual old age in humans. You add extra organs, limbs, and… pretty much just various superpowers.

And then you have the O5 say things like:

They have minds and personalities better suited to long periods of solitude; they don't need the large social systems that we do, to keep ourselves from going crazy. We're children compared to them.

They're us, as we're meant to be: apex predators, without society, without civilization. Our bodies want us to be brutal individuals who only come together to mate and fight.

The two problems with this are:

1) The O5's worshipful tone. He/she is clearly idolizing these creatures. "We're children compared to them! They're us, as we were meant to be!"

2) It's patent nonsense, which is not a problem (again, I wrote SCP-1000!), but the kind of patent nonsense that breaks my suspension of disbelief (which is a problem).

This is at least in part thanks to my partial anthroplogy background. Our bodies are calculated towards being ultra-social; our entire biology points in that direction. There are solitary, monogamous apes (and humans aren't actually monogamous from a biological perspective - I'm not gonna touch morality here or whatever): they're gibbons. Gibbons live in isolation, have tiny genitalia, and mate very little. They don't have civilizations. They're not apex predators, though - and this is more nonsense, because there's no reason apex predators can't be social, and these super-humans ARE social. Sociopaths are still social. So they're clearly "evolved" to prey on … well, primarily their own children. There is no reasonable mechanism in existence for doing so. (Niven bullshits this one pretty well too by providing a purpose and outside mechanism for the third form; but he doesn't go for the predation aspect).

So basically, you've taken people, pasted on a completely random set of superpowers (Niven's weren't random), made them nasty predators, and said 'this is how we were meant to be'. Meant by whom? Certainly not nature. Maybe there's an "aliens did it" (or "we are descended from aliens" ala Niven) backstory to this too, but… I dunno. I still don't think that would work for me. Eh.

edit: Also, did you have to make them Evil Fat People? Man. I doubt it's what you were intentionally going for, but… Man.

Ultimately, I'm sorry to say this doesn't do it for me. Niven did this exact same thing but much better. Doesn't mean you can't do it too, but you need to outdo Niven. And even ignoring Pak Protectors, the method of execution here is is still too cheesy for me and also breaks my suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They're four-armed 40K Space Marines. Except fat. And they eat kids. So basically the same except four arms and fat.

Still, I like this. It's a nice twist on that feeling of disgust and pity most people feel when seeing someone who is morbidly obese. It turns it on its ear, though, by hypothesizing that the 'poor, pathetic' obese guy may actually be a cunning predator using that same feeling as a tool to eat children.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I said: It's not impossible. But the leadership aren't going to go "Oh, that's clearly our natural final stage" because, well, it's not.

If it's our final stage; it's not natural, and given that it's not natural it's likely that something else is involved.

The note at the end could, conceivably, be correct. The problem is, even if it IS correct, the O5s wouldn't believe it without some amazingly good evidence, which would be mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with thee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also the fact that humans are neotenous, and well aware of it; we're apes that maintain a lot of childlike features.

Well, there's nothing to say that a neotenous species couldn't further evolve a metamorphosis into a adult form different from the ancestral species, and then have the environment change so the metamorphosis isn't triggered, making it neotenous in two different ways.

Adult forms aren't suddenly going to develop lots of redundant organs,

If the metamorphosis and/or final adult form is truly paranormal, rather than merely normal but unexplained, then humanity went through some sort of paranormal evolutionary process, in which case any sort of weird thing could manifest in adulthood.
